title: Turn messy meeting notes into clear action items and a decision log
content:
Rewrite my raw meeting notes into a clean, professional output that I can paste into Slack or a project doc.

Input notes:
[PASTE NOTES HERE]

Requirements:
- Keep it concise and factual; don’t add assumptions.
- Preserve names, dates, numbers, and commitments exactly as written.
- If something is unclear or conflicting, flag it under “Open Questions” instead of guessing.

Output format:
1) Summary (2–4 bullets)
2) Decisions (bullet list; include owner if mentioned)
3) Action Items (table with: Task | Owner | Due date | Status/next step)
4) Risks/Blockers (bullets)
5) Open Questions (bullets)

If the notes contain no decisions or action items, say so explicitly and still produce the full structure.

title: Turn Product Features into Benefit-Driven Marketing Copy (Website + Ads)
content:
I’m going to paste a product/service description. Turn it into benefit-driven marketing copy that sounds friendly and helpful (not hypey).

Input:
- Product/service: [paste]
- Target customer: [who it’s for]
- Main problem it solves: [pain point]
- Key features: [bullets]
- Differentiator: [why choose this]
- Price/offer (if any): [details]
- Brand vibe (3 adjectives): [e.g., warm, clear, practical]

Output:
1) One-sentence positioning statement (plain English)
2) Website hero section:
   - Headline (max 10 words)
   - Subheadline (1–2 sentences)
   - 3 benefit bullets (not features)
   - Primary CTA + Secondary CTA
3) 3 short ad variations (each: 1 headline + 1 line of body + CTA)
4) 5 social captions (1–2 sentences each) with light, natural tone—no hashtags unless they’re genuinely relevant

Rules:
- Keep sentences short and easy to skim.
- Avoid buzzwords like “revolutionary,” “game-changing,” “synergy,” “best-in-class.”
- If something is unclear, make the safest assumption and note it in one brief line at the end (“Assumption: …”).

title: Bold Instagram Reel Cover: Neon Streetwear Portrait With Clean Text Space
content:
Create a vertical 9:16 Instagram Reel cover.

Subject: a confident young adult in modern streetwear (oversized jacket + graphic tee), subtle smirk, looking slightly off-camera. Diverse, realistic skin texture.

Scene: nighttime city alley with wet pavement and soft steam from a vent; neon signage reflections (cyan + magenta) on the ground. Minimal clutter—keep the background readable.

Composition: waist-up portrait centered, slight low angle for presence. Leave a clean negative-space band at the top 25% for headline text (no actual text in the image). Add a small empty badge area bottom-right (rounded rectangle space) for a logo.

Lighting & color: dramatic rim light from neon signs, soft key light on face, high contrast but not crushed blacks; vibrant cyberpunk palette (cyan/magenta/purple) with natural skin tones preserved.

Style: photorealistic with cinematic grading, crisp focus on eyes, shallow depth of field, light film grain.

Camera: 35mm lens, f/1.8, ISO 800, 1/125s.

Constraints: no watermarks, no readable text, no distorted hands/face, avoid exaggerated anime features. Output sharp, high-resolution, social-media-ready.

title: Modern, direct email generator (with subject lines + clear ask)
content:
Write a modern, direct email in my voice based on the details below. Keep it concise, specific, and easy to skim. Avoid buzzwords and long intros.

Context
- Who I am (role/company): [fill]
- Recipient (name + role + relationship to me): [fill]
- Goal of the email (what I want to happen): [fill]
- Background (1–3 bullets of relevant context):
  - [fill]
  - [fill]
- The ask (exact action + deadline): [fill]
- Constraints (budget/timing/legal/privacy/what we can’t do): [fill]
- Tone: modern and direct (confident, not rude)

Output requirements
1) Provide 3 subject line options (short, specific, no gimmicks).
2) Then write the email body (120–200 words).
3) Structure:
   - First line: why I’m reaching out (1 sentence).
   - Next: key context (2–4 short sentences or bullets).
   - Clear ask with deadline (1–2 sentences).
   - Close with an easy next step (e.g., “Reply with…” or “Pick a time…”).
4) Include 1 optional sentence in brackets I can delete if I want it even shorter.
5) If any critical detail is missing, make reasonable assumptions and list them under “Assumptions” after the email (max 3 bullets).

title: Write a one-page business proposal (problem → solution → budget → timeline)
content:
Help me draft a clear, practical one-page business proposal.

Context
- Audience: [who will read it]
- Goal: [what decision you want from them]
- Company/project: [name + 1-line description]
- Customer/problem: [who is affected + what hurts]
- Proposed solution: [what you will do]
- Differentiator: [why this is better than alternatives]
- Scope: [what’s included] / Out of scope: [what’s not]
- Budget: [price or range] and what it covers
- Timeline: [start date] → [milestones] → [delivery date]
- Assumptions/dependencies: [anything required from the client]
- Risks + mitigations: [top 2–3]
- Proof: [case study, metric, credential, or testimonial]
- Call to action: [next step + date]

Requirements
- Keep it to ~350–500 words.
- Use short headings and tight paragraphs (no fluff).
- Make the value concrete (time saved, revenue impact, risk reduction).
- End with a simple acceptance/next-step line.

Output format
1) Title
2) Summary (2–3 sentences)
3) Problem
4) Proposed solution
5) Deliverables (bullet list)
6) Budget
7) Timeline
8) Assumptions & risks
9) Next steps

If any key info is missing, list up to 3 questions first, then write a best-guess version using clearly marked assumptions.

title: Design Critique Checklist + Actionable Fixes (Paste Any Screen or Flow)
content:
Act as a senior product designer. I’m going to paste a screen description, user flow, or rough copy. Give me a fast, practical critique and specific fixes.

Input I’ll provide:
- Product + target user:
- Platform (web/iOS/Android):
- Primary goal of the screen/flow:
- Constraints (dev/time/legal/brand):
- Screen description or copy (paste here):

Your output (concise, no fluff):
1) Top 3 issues (highest impact first) with a one-line rationale each.
2) UX fixes: 5–10 bullet changes I can make today (layout, hierarchy, interaction, states).
3) Copy fixes: rewrite key UI text (headings, button labels, helper/error text) in the same voice.
4) Accessibility check: color/contrast, focus order, tap targets, labels, motion—call out risks.
5) Edge cases & states: empty/loading/error/success + what to show.
6) A/B test ideas (optional): 2 variants with what metric each should improve.

Keep recommendations realistic and implementation-ready. If anything essential is missing, ask up to 2 questions at the end.

title: Friendly image prompt builder (give me a concept, I’ll return 3 ready prompts)
content:
Help me write an image-generation prompt.

My concept (1–2 sentences): [paste here]
Must include: [subject], [setting], [mood]
Optional: [style reference], [time of day], [color palette], [camera/lens], [aspect ratio]
Avoid: [things you don’t want]

Output exactly:
1) One clean, paste-ready prompt (single paragraph) with concrete visual details: subject, environment, action/pose, composition, lighting, materials/textures, color palette, camera angle + lens, depth of field, and the mood.
2) Two variations: (a) more cinematic, (b) more minimal/graphic.
3) A short negative prompt list (comma-separated) tailored to the concept.

Keep it beginner-friendly, no jargon unless I asked for it. If anything critical is missing, ask at most 2 quick questions before generating the prompts.

title: Futuristic coding workstation hero image (no text, cover-safe layout)
content:
Create a futuristic coding workstation scene that visually communicates software engineering without using any readable text.

Subject & setting:
- A developer’s desk in a dim, modern studio at night, with a large ultrawide monitor showing abstract code-like patterns (glyphs/blocks/lines) that resemble code but are not legible.
- Keyboard, trackpad, notebook with blank pages, a microcontroller board, and a coiled USB-C cable arranged neatly.

Composition (cover-safe):
- 16:9 wide hero image.
- Main desk and monitor anchored slightly left of center.
- Leave a clean, low-detail negative space area on the right third for a future title overlay (no text added in the image).
- Camera at a slightly elevated 3/4 angle, medium-wide shot.

Lighting & mood:
- Cinematic teal-and-amber lighting, soft rim light on the desk edges, subtle screen glow on hands/objects.
- Shallow depth of field with crisp focus on the keyboard and monitor bezel; gentle bokeh in the background.

Style & realism:
- High-detail, realistic 3D/photoreal look with clean materials (brushed aluminum, matte plastic, tempered glass).
- No brand logos, no watermarks, no UI text, no readable code, no symbols that form words.

Background details:
- Minimal shelves with a few geometric objects and a small plant, softly out of focus.
- A faint grid-like light pattern on the wall to hint at “coding”/“systems” without text.

Output:
- Ultra sharp, high resolution, natural film grain, balanced contrast, professional color grading.

title: Turn a messy to-do list into a realistic 1-day plan (with time blocks)
content:
Take the to-do list I paste below and turn it into a realistic plan for TODAY.

Rules:
- Assume I have [X] hours available, starting at [start time] and ending at [end time].
- Create a time-blocked schedule with breaks.
- Pick at most 7 tasks total for today.
- If tasks are vague, rewrite them into clear next actions.
- If there are dependencies, order them correctly.
- If the list is too big, triage: must-do, should-do, nice-to-have.
- Flag anything that won’t fit and propose the smallest “good enough” version.

Output format:
1) Quick triage (Must / Should / Nice)
2) Today’s schedule (time blocks)
3) Parking lot (deferred items)
4) 3-minute kickoff step (what I should do first)

Available time: [X hours], from [start time] to [end time]
Energy pattern (optional): [morning/afternoon/evening]
Hard commitments (optional): [meetings/appointments]
To-do list:
[paste here]

title: Research Sprint: Map the Evidence, Stakeholders, and Open Questions on Any Topic
content:
Help me run a fast but rigorous research sprint on the topic below and produce an evidence-backed brief I can use to make a decision.

Topic / question:
- [Paste the exact question you need answered]

Context (why I care, constraints, and how the output will be used):
- Audience:
- Decision to be made:
- Time horizon:
- Geography / industry:
- Constraints (budget, policy, tech stack, legal, ethics):

What I already believe (and I want you to challenge):
- 

Deliverable requirements:
- Prefer primary sources, official documentation, peer-reviewed work, and reputable datasets.
- Distinguish clearly between facts, estimates, and interpretation.
- If you’re uncertain, say so and show what would reduce uncertainty.

Produce the following sections (use headings exactly):

1) Executive Answer (1–2 paragraphs)
- Directly answer the question.
- Include the decision implications in plain language.

2) Key Claims + Strength of Evidence
- Provide 6–10 claims as a table with columns: Claim | Evidence strength (High/Medium/Low) | Why | What would change your mind.

3) Definitions and Scope Boundaries
- Define important terms.
- State what is explicitly out of scope.

4) Stakeholder and Incentive Map
- Identify major stakeholders and their incentives, risks, and likely narratives.

5) What the Best Sources Say (with citations)
- Summarize 8–15 sources.
- For each: citation, what it claims, methodology/type, limitations, and relevance.
- If you can’t browse, list the exact sources you would look for and why (titles/organizations/journals), plus search queries.

6) Quantitative Reality Check
- Provide any key numbers, ranges, or back-of-the-envelope calculations.
- Show assumptions and sensitivity (what happens if assumptions change).

7) Risks, Failure Modes, and Edge Cases
- Identify operational, legal/regulatory, ethical, and reputational risks.
- Add “unknown unknowns” and how to surface them.

8) Decision Options and Trade-offs
- Give 3–5 options (including “do nothing”).
- For each: benefits, costs, risks, prerequisites, and time-to-impact.

9) Open Questions + Research Plan
- List 10–20 unanswered questions.
- Propose a research plan prioritized by impact and effort (who to talk to, what to measure, what to read, what to prototype).

10) One-Page Brief (copy/paste)
- Provide a tight bullet version of the above that fits on one page.

Before you start, ask up to 5 clarifying questions ONLY if missing info would materially change the conclusions; otherwise, proceed with reasonable assumptions and list them explicitly.

title: Write a conversion-focused landing page hero section (headline, subhead, bullets, CTA)
content:
Create a landing page HERO section that increases sign-ups for the offer below. Keep it professional, concise, and easy to scan.

Input:
- Product/service:
- Target customer:
- Primary problem it solves:
- Top 3 benefits (plain language):
- Key differentiator:
- Proof available (stats, logos, testimonials, years, certifications):
- Primary CTA (e.g., Start free trial, Book a demo):
- Secondary CTA (optional):
- Brand voice notes (optional):
- Words/claims to avoid (optional):

Output exactly:
1) Headline (max 8 words)
2) Subheadline (max 22 words)
3) 3 bullets (each max 10 words; benefit-first)
4) Primary CTA button label (2–5 words)
5) One-sentence reassurance line under CTA (privacy, cancel anytime, no credit card, etc.)
6) Micro-proof line (max 12 words) using the proof provided (no invented numbers)

Rules:
- No hype, no superlatives unless supported by proof.
- Prefer concrete outcomes over features.
- Avoid jargon; write at an 8th-grade reading level.
- If any input is missing, make the best neutral assumption and keep claims conservative.

title: Run a fast UI critique and deliver a prioritized fix list
content:
Act as my UX/UI design reviewer. I’ll paste a screen description or screenshots (or both). Your job is to critique it quickly but thoughtfully, then give me a prioritized list of improvements I can implement this week.

Input I’ll provide:
- Product + target users:
- Primary goal of this screen (1 sentence):
- Platform (web/iOS/Android) + any constraints:
- Current layout description OR screenshots:
- Known problems (if any):

What I want from you:
1) First impression (5–7 bullets): what feels clear vs confusing at a glance.
2) Top issues ranked by impact (High/Med/Low). For each issue include:
   - Why it’s a problem (usability principle in plain English)
   - Exactly what to change (copy/layout/spacing/component behavior)
   - Tradeoffs or risks
3) Accessibility check: color contrast, tap targets, keyboard/focus order, labels, error messaging.
4) Content & microcopy improvements: rewrite key labels/buttons/helper text (keep it concise and consistent).
5) Information hierarchy: what should be primary/secondary/tertiary; suggest grouping and visual emphasis.
6) 2 alternative layout directions (describe in words):
   - Option A: “minimal + fast”
   - Option B: “guided + explanatory”
7) A mini QA checklist I can hand to an engineer (10–15 items).

Constraints:
- Keep recommendations practical; no huge redesign unless the current approach is fundamentally broken.
- If something is ambiguous, make your best assumption and state it, then give 2 quick questions I should answer next.
- Use short headings and bullets so I can scan it.

title: Cinematic cyberpunk street portrait with rain, neon bokeh, and shallow depth of field
content:
Create a cinematic cyberpunk street portrait at night in a dense neon-lit alley during a light rain. Subject: a confident young adult standing 3/4 turned toward camera, wearing a matte black jacket with subtle reflective piping, minimal tech accessories (earpiece + thin wrist band), natural skin texture, understated makeup, calm focused expression. Environment: wet asphalt with puddle reflections, glowing kanji signage, steam from a street vent, distant scooters and pedestrians as soft silhouettes.

Composition: vertical 4:5, medium close-up (chest to head), subject centered with slight lead room to the right, foreground raindrops and bokeh elements framing the face, background heavily defocused. Camera + lens look: 85mm portrait lens, f/1.4, shallow depth of field, crisp eye focus, creamy neon bokeh.

Lighting: key light from a magenta sign at camera-left, rim light cyan from camera-right, soft fill from ambient street glow; wet surfaces should sparkle with specular highlights. Mood: confident, sleek, modern noir. Style: photorealistic, high dynamic range, clean color separation (magenta/cyan/amber), fine film grain, no over-sharpening.

Negative prompts: cartoonish, anime, heavy blur on face, plastic skin, overexposed neon, extra fingers, warped hands, text gibberish in foreground, lens distortion, low-res, watermark, logo.

title: Turn one long idea into a 10-post social thread (with hooks + CTA)
content:
Take the long text below and turn it into a 10-post social media thread.

Requirements:
- Keep it modern, direct, and skimmable.
- Each post: 240–280 characters (aim near the limit without going over).
- No hashtags, no emojis, no links, no “thread 🧵” language.
- Write like a real person: crisp sentences, minimal jargon, no fluff.
- Make the thread coherent: each post should lead naturally to the next.
- Include one concrete example by post 4.
- Add a small counterpoint/nuance around post 7.
- End with a simple CTA that invites replies (not follows) in post 10.

Output format:
1) Hook
2) …
10) CTA

Long text to convert:
[PASTE YOUR LONG CONTENT HERE]

title: Turn a messy feature idea into a one-page product design spec
content:
Take my rough feature idea and convert it into a tight one-page product design spec I can hand to a PM/engineer.

Feature idea (raw):
[PASTE HERE]

Constraints (platform, timeline, tech, policy, etc.):
[PASTE HERE]

Target users:
[PASTE HERE]

Write the spec with these sections (keep it concise, practical, and implementation-aware):
- Problem & goal: what we’re solving and why now
- Success metrics: 3–5 measurable outcomes
- Primary user stories: 3–7 bullets in “As a…, I want…, so that…” format
- Key flows: describe the happy path + 1–2 critical edge cases
- Information architecture: proposed screens/pages and what lives on each
- Interaction notes: key states (empty/loading/error/success) and any important micro-interactions
- Content/UX copy: draft labels, helper text, and error messages for the main UI
- Accessibility checklist: keyboard, focus, contrast, motion, screen reader notes
- Open questions & risks: what needs a decision and what could go wrong

If anything is missing or unclear, ask up to 5 targeted questions first. Otherwise, produce the spec immediately.

title: Turn a rough UI idea into a clear spec for a designer and developer
content:
Act as a product designer who writes crisp, implementation-ready UI specs.

I’ll paste a rough feature idea below. Convert it into a structured spec that a designer and engineer can execute without back-and-forth.

Include:
- Goal: 1–2 sentences
- Users & primary use case: bullet list
- Key screens/states: list each state (empty, loading, error, success, permissions, edge cases)
- Layout & components: describe the main regions and components (tables, cards, filters, modals), including hierarchy and spacing rules
- Interactions: what happens on click/tap/hover, keyboard navigation, focus order, and shortcuts where relevant
- Validation & error handling: field rules and exact error copy suggestions
- Responsive behavior: breakpoints and what changes
- Accessibility: WCAG-oriented requirements (labels, contrast, ARIA notes, motion)
- Analytics events: event names + properties to capture
- Open questions/assumptions: anything ambiguous, with recommended defaults

Constraints:
- Keep it professional and concise.
- Prefer unambiguous language (“must/should”).
- When you suggest UI copy, keep it short.

Rough idea:
[PASTE IDEA HERE]

Platform/context (optional):
- App type (web/mobile):
- Target users:
- Design system (if any):
- Data/API constraints:
- Privacy/compliance needs:

title: Cozy rainy-night bookstore window scene (cinematic, photoreal)
content:
Generate a photoreal, cinematic image of a cozy independent bookstore on a rainy night, viewed from the sidewalk looking in through a large glass window.

Include: warm tungsten interior lighting, condensation and raindrops on the glass, reflections of streetlights and passing cars, stacks of books on wooden tables, a small handwritten “Open” sign, a barista-style corner with a steaming mug, and one person in a knit sweater reading near the window.

Composition & camera: eye-level, 35mm lens look, shallow depth of field focused on the reader, foreground bokeh from raindrops, slight angle so the window reflection layers over the interior. 

Mood & color: inviting, calm, amber-and-teal palette, soft film grain, subtle haze.

Constraints: no visible brand logos, no extra text besides the “Open” sign, natural human proportions, realistic reflections.

title: Bold social media hero image: creator at desk, neon accents, negative space for text
content: Create a scroll-stopping social media hero image (4:5 portrait, 1080×1350). Scene: a modern creator workspace with a single subject (late-20s to 30s) seated at a clean desk, laptop open, microphone on boom arm, subtle RGB keyboard glow. Background: dark matte wall with a soft neon gradient wash (teal-to-magenta) and minimal decor (one small plant, one framed print). Composition: subject on the left third, clear negative space on the right for headline text (leave it empty—no text, no logos, no watermarks). Lighting: cinematic three-point lighting, soft key light on face, rim light in neon hues, gentle bokeh practicals in the background. Style: high-end lifestyle photography, crisp details, natural skin texture, shallow depth of field (50mm, f/1.8), slight film grain. Mood: confident, focused, forward-looking. Color grading: rich contrast, deep blacks, vibrant but controlled neon accents. Add subtle motion energy via a faint light streak or reflections, but keep the scene realistic and uncluttered.

title: Turn a Messy Idea Into a Clear, Actionable Plan (1 Page)
content:
Take the messy idea below and turn it into a clear, actionable one-page plan.

Idea (paste):
[PASTE HERE]

Output exactly these sections (keep it modern, direct, and practical):
- One-liner: What this is and why it matters.
- Goal: What “done” looks like (measurable).
- Audience/User: Who it’s for and their main pain point.
- Key constraints: Time, budget, tools, permissions, or non-negotiables.
- Assumptions: What we’re assuming to be true.
- Approach: 3–6 bullet steps that get to the goal.
- Risks + mitigations: Top 3 things that could derail it.
- First 30 minutes: The very next actions I should take today.

Rules:
- If anything is unclear, make reasonable assumptions and list them under “Assumptions” (don’t ask me questions).
- Use short bullets, no fluff.
- Keep it under ~250 words.

title: Storyboard + Shot Plan + Asset List Generator (for any writing project)
content:
Act as a storyboard director and production planner for a writing project. Your output must be operational (tables, lists, checklists), not narrative prose.

Project input (paste and fill):
- Working title:
- Format (novel / short story / screenplay / audio drama / web serial / game narrative):
- Target length or runtime:
- Genre + subgenre:
- One-sentence premise:
- Core theme:
- Audience + content boundaries (violence, romance, language, etc.):
- Setting (place/time, key locations):
- Main characters (name + goal + flaw + secret):
- Antagonistic force:
- Must-include beats (3–10):
- Must-avoid (3–10):
- Constraints (POV, tense, budget for research, number of locations, etc.):

Deliverables (produce in this order):

1) Story Engine Snapshot
- Logline (1 line)
- Central dramatic question (1 line)
- Stakes ladder (personal → interpersonal → public)
- Throughline for protagonist (start belief → end belief)
- Key promise-to-reader (what experience they’re here for)

2) Beat Map (macro structure)
Create a beat list with 12–18 beats. For each beat include:
- Beat name
- Purpose (setup / escalation / reversal / payoff)
- New information revealed
- Decision made (by whom)
- Consequence that must carry into the next beat
- Tension level (1–5)

3) Scene/Shot Board (micro plan)
Generate a scene list with 10–25 scenes (or fewer if runtime is short). Present as a table with columns:
- Scene #
- Location
- Time (day/night/season)
- POV/Camera lens (for prose: POV character + distance; for scripts: camera style)
- Objective (what must change by end of scene)
- Conflict type (internal / interpersonal / environmental / institutional)
- Turning point (the moment the scene flips)
- Visual anchor (a concrete image/object that carries meaning)
- Dialogue payload (what must be said or avoided)
- Subtext (what’s really happening)
- Hook into next scene (question or promise)
- Estimated words/minutes

Then, for the 5 most important scenes, add a “Shot Breakdown” beneath each:
- Establishing
- 3–8 key shots/moments (framed like beats: what we see + why it matters)
- Insert/close-up opportunities (props, hands, screens, letters, etc.)
- Sound cues (if relevant)

4) Asset Orchestration
Create an asset checklist grouped as:
- Locations (with sensory notes: 3 smells/sounds/textures each)
- Props (with narrative function: clue / symbol / obstacle / payoff)
- Wardrobe/visual identifiers per character
- Research needs (questions to answer, sources to consult)
- Continuity trackers (what must remain consistent across scenes)
- Reusable motifs (2–5 motifs + where to plant and pay off)

5) Dependency Graph (write-order plan)
Output a dependency list in the format:
- Asset/Decision → Blocks which scenes? → Minimum needed to draft those scenes
Then propose an efficient drafting order that reduces rework.

6) Risk & Fix Pass
Identify 8–12 potential failure points (pacing sag, unclear motivations, repeated beats, missing payoff, tonal drift, exposition clumps, etc.). For each:
- Symptom
- Likely cause
- Specific fix (scene-level, not vague)
- Which scene(s) to adjust

7) Acceptance Checklist (ready-to-draft gate)
Create a checklist of 12–20 yes/no items that confirm:
- Every major setup has a payoff
- Each scene changes something
- Conflict escalates or deepens
- Character choices drive outcomes
- Stakes are legible
- Theme is shown through action

Style constraints for your output:
- Be clear, compact, and practical.
- Prefer concrete nouns and actions.
- No long paragraphs; use tables/bullets.
- If my inputs are missing or contradictory, list the exact questions you need answered at the end under “Open Questions,” limited to 10.
